text: The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein and medializes the small bowel to the right side of the patient. The mesentery is entered in a medial to lateral approach beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric artery on the screen's right. The mesentery, a fat layer containing blood vessels, is dissected away from the retroperitoneum, revealing structures like the left kidney, gonadal vessels, and ureter beneath.

The surgeon separates tissue planes to mobilize the mesentery, tenting up the mesentery of the transverse colon directly above the inferior mesenteric vein. Side branches from the middle colic vessel are identified, and the lesser sac is entered above the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein is divided to facilitate mesentery mobilization towards the pelvis.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to divide the inferior mesenteric vein. The dissection continues, revealing and dividing an arc of Rhyolan to facilitate reach. The mobilization is completed underneath the mesentery, extending all the way to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral attachments of the colon from the splenic flexure and distal transverse colon, holding up the omental fat. The spleen is visible in the corner of the view. The omental attachments to the transverse colon are being finished to ensure adequate reach into the pelvis.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvic region, identifying the right iliac artery with the right ureter crossing over it and the gonadal vessels visible. The base of the mesentery of the sigmoid or recto-sigmoid colon is scored, entering the avascular plane characterized by a wispy layer. The superior rectal artery is held up for better visualization.

The surgeon visualizes the left iliac vessel, left ureter, and left gonadal vessels in a thin patient, facilitating clear anatomical identification. The inferior mesenteric artery, the main blood supply to the left colon and upper rectum, is isolated for high ligation near the aorta. The aorta and iliac vessels are visible, and the mesentery is separated from retroperitoneal structures, including the ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon takes down the remaining lateral attachments of the abdominal portion of the colon, completing the mobilization. The focus then shifts towards the pelvis, approaching the upper rectum. Black ink is visible, marking the level of the tumor that was tattooed prior to surgery.

The surgeon enters the avascular holy plane behind the rectum, dissecting along the sacrum visible at the floor of the view. The dissection continues down to the pelvic floor muscles for a complete resection, and then proceeds anteriorly to separate the rectum from the seminal vesicles and prostate.

The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular wispy plane around the rectum, progressing down towards the pelvic floor. The plane appears slightly distorted due to the presence of ink marking the tumor level.

The surgeon inserts a robotic linear cutting stapler with two rows of staples and a blade in between, using a 45-millimeter length stapler to seal the rectum on either end, preventing any spillage into the surgical field.

The surgeon pulls the tumor-containing section of the colon out of the pelvis, leaving the pelvis empty after rectum removal. The mesentery is separated up to the proximal colon, where reconnection to the rectum will occur. Endocyanin green dye is injected, and laser imaging reveals areas with good blood supply as green.
The surgeon grasps the end of the colon and prepares to complete the procedure through a small incision, indicating the transition from robotic to open surgical technique.
